title: Chapter 12: The Vantage Point
content: The phone rang at 9:47 AM.

Aline knew it wasn't Cass before she looked at the screen. Cass didn't call at predictable hours. He appeared in the corners of her life like weather — sudden, unavoidable, and entirely on his own schedule. This was something else. The prefix was corporate: 323-555-VANT. Vantage Records.

She should have let it go to voicemail. Every instinct she'd honed in the industry told her to let it ring, to control the terms of engagement, to never answer a call she hadn't initiated. But her thumb was already sliding across the screen.
"Aline Rivers." The voice on the other end was warm — furnace-warm, the kind of warmth that radiated from structures built to hide something cold. "This is Marcus Webb."
Just like that. No assistant, no gatekeeper, no preamble about how he'd gotten her number. As if calling her directly was the most natural thing in the world.

"I've been listening to you on repeat for two weeks," he said, and she could hear the smile in it — not the predatory leer she'd expected from everything Cass had implied, but something practiced and easy, a laugh held just beneath the surface. "Your voice, your production choices. The architecture of those tracks. Someone taught you how to build tension like nobody else in this city."
Her pulse flickered. She sat straighter on the edge of Cass's bed — the Nest's bed, she corrected herself, though the distinction was wearing thin.
"Can we have coffee?" Marcus continued. "My office, tomorrow, ten AM. Nothing formal. I just want to talk."
She should say no. She should run it past Cass first. She should do a hundred things besides what she actually did, which was hear her own voice say: "I'll think about it."
Marcus Webb laughed — that practiced, easy laugh again. "Fair enough. I'll have my assistant send the details. Just in case." The line clicked clean.
Aline held the phone away from her ear and stared at it like it might bite.

She found Cass in the studio alcove, headphones around his neck, one hand resting on the mixing board. The track he'd been building overnight — that bright, strange thing that had startled her awake — was pulled across the timeline in amber waves. He looked up when she entered, and she watched the read of her face happen in real time. Cass missed nothing.
"Someone called," she said.

His posture didn't change. His stillness did. It shifted from the stillness of concentration to something else — the stillness before a storm, the way the air compressed in the seconds before lightning cracks.
"Who?"
"Marcus Webb."

The name landed in the room like a stone dropped into still water. Ripples moved outward across Cass's features — tightening jaw, narrowing eyes, the tendons in his neck pulling taut. He pulled the headphones off and set them down with exaggerated care, as though he might break something if he moved too fast.
"I asked you not to talk to him."
"You asked." Aline crossed her arms. "You didn't forbid."

His jaw worked. The muscle at the hinge of his cheekbone flexed once, twice. She waited for the argument — for the walls to come up, for him to pull her back into the Nest's gravity and seal the doors. For a full five seconds, she thought he would.
Instead: "Then go."
The words came out flat, stripped of everything. No permission, no blessing. Just fact.

"But understand what you're walking into." He turned to face her fully, and his eyes were dark wells with something moving at the bottom — something that looked, impossibly, like fear. "Marcus Webb doesn't sign artists. He collects them. And when they're no longer useful, he shelves them. Permanently. I've seen it happen to people with ten times your talent and none of your instinct. He'll make you feel like you're the only person who matters, and by the time you realize you're not, he'll already own everything you came in with."

The anger was there — she could hear it thrumming beneath the words — but it wasn't directed at her. It was old anger, calcified and razor-edged, aimed at a target Cass had been circling for a long time.

And beneath the anger: fear. Cass, who moved through the deep web like water through cracks, who traced digital ghosts for a living and never flinched, was afraid. She saw it in the way his hands wouldn't quite stabilize, in the way he looked at her not with suspicion but with something closer to a warning he didn't know how to deliver.

She almost asked him why. Almost pressed her palm flat against that fear and demanded its source. But the question would have to wait, because tomorrow at ten AM, she was walking into Vantage Tower, and she needed to be clearheaded when she did.
The elevator took two minutes.

She timed it on her phone, watching the numbers climb — 1, 2, 5, 12, 20, 30 — while the car hummed around her and the city fell away below. By the time the display read PENTHOUSE, her stomach had adjusted to the altitude and her pulse had settled into something manageable. She'd dressed carefully: dark gray blazer over a black tee, minimal jewelry, her hair pulled back tight. Professional. Unreadable. At least that was the hope.

The doors opened into an anteroom that made the word obsolete. This wasn't a waiting area; it was a statement. Polished concrete floors, a Rothko on the far wall — real, she was almost certain, which meant it cost more than every asset she'd ever owned combined — and floor-to-ceiling windows that turned the entire city into a diorama. From up here, the streets were veins and the buildings were teeth and everything looked small enough to pick up and rearrange.
A woman in a charcoal dress rose from a desk that seemed to float. "Ms. Rivers. Mr. Webb will see you now."
No waiting. No cool-your-heels power play. He was better than that. He didn't need to make her wait; he needed her to come to him, and she had.

The inner office was more glass, more view, more money distilled into architecture. The air smelled of sandalwood and bergamot — expensive, the kind of expensive that didn't announce itself so much as infiltrate. The perfume of a man who understood that presence started before you entered the room.

Marcus Webb stood from behind a desk made of a single slab of black walnut and came toward her with his hand extended. He was taller than she'd expected from photos, broader through the shoulders, with silver threading through dark hair at the temples in a way that looked deliberate. His suit was charcoal, open-collared, no tie. He dressed like a man who didn't need the armor.
"Aline." He took her hand. His grip was firm and cool — stone-cold, the kind of cold that seeped through skin and settled in the bone. "You're even more impressive in person."

His smile was perfect. Not blinding, not flashy — perfect. The kind of smile that made you feel like you'd earned something just by receiving it. She let him hold her hand one beat longer than was comfortable, watching his eyes. They were gray-green, and they were cataloging her. Every detail, every micro-expression, fed into some internal engine she couldn't see.

"Thank you for making the time," he said, gesturing toward a seating area with leather chairs and a low table where coffee was already poured. Steam curled from two cups. He'd known she'd come. Of course he had.

She sat. The leather was butter-soft, yielding beneath her in a way that felt like the chair was learning her shape. Marcus settled across from her, close enough that she could see the grain of his shave, the fine lines around his eyes that deepened when he smiled.

"I'll be direct," he said. "I want to work with you. Full creative control. Whatever vision you're building — and I've been listening closely enough to know there's a vision — you keep it. No A&R interference, no focus-grouping your sound into something marketable, no committee." He leaned forward slightly. "What I offer is distribution. The best distribution infrastructure on the continent. Label-independent, but with all the muscle. Your music, your call. My network, my resources."
It was, on paper, an extraordinary offer. The kind of deal that didn't exist anymore, if it ever had. Aline let the words settle, let her face stay neutral.

"And mentorship," Marcus added. His voice softened a degree — not less warm, but more intimate, as though he were letting her glimpse something private. "I see something in you that reminds me of someone I used to work with."
Her fingers went still around the coffee cup she hadn't lifted.
"Someone extraordinary," he continued. "Who disappeared."
The silence that followed was absolute. No street noise this high up, no ambient hum. Just her breath and his and the weight of those two words — *who disappeared* — hanging in the bergamot air.

Her pulse spiked. She felt it in her throat, in her temples, in the sudden heat at the backs of her knees. Did he know? Was this a probe, a test, a noose already tightening? Cass's name sat in her mouth like a stone she couldn't swallow and couldn't spit out.

Marcus watched her. His gray-green eyes didn't flicker, didn't waver. He was reading her — reading the skip in her breathing, the way her thumb pressed harder against the ceramic cup, the fractional widening of her pupils. Like a hawk watches a field mouse, still and patient, certain of the outcome before the dive.
His smile never wavered.
"That sounds like a complicated history," she managed.

"It is." He sat back, and the moment — the crack in the ice — sealed over as though it had never opened. "But I don't trade in history. I trade in potential. And yours, Aline, is extraordinary. The word isn't chosen lightly."
He slid a folder across the table. Black cover, no label. "The terms are inside. No rush. Take your time, read them with whoever you trust. I'm a patient man."

She picked up the folder. The paper was heavy, expensive, the kind of stock that felt like it would outlast whatever was printed on it. She stood, and Marcus rose with her — a courtesy that felt less like manners and more like a predator standing to see the prey off the territory.
"It was a pleasure, Aline. Truly."
"The pleasure was mine." The words came out steady, which was a small miracle.
His handshake again: stone-cold, precise, lingering one beat too long.
The elevator doors closed, and she was alone with the hum of the descent and the black folder in her hands. Her phone vibrated against her thigh.
Cass: "What did he say?"
She typed with thumbs that weren't quite steady: "He mentioned someone who disappeared. Someone extraordinary."

The read receipt appeared instantly. Then nothing. The elevator counted down — 30, 20, 12 — and the silence from Cass stretched like wire. She could feel it tightening across the distance between them, could almost hear him on the other end, sitting in the Nest with his jaw locked and his hands flat on the desk, fighting the urge to tear the city apart looking for her.
Then: "Come home."
Not *come back*. Not *where are you* or *get out of there*. *Come home.*

She read it twice. The word sat in the center of her chest — *home* — and it was too big, too warm, too heavy. The Nest. His space, his domain, his fortress of screens and shadows and sound. Home. The word terrified her. The word comforted her. Both things were true at once, and both things were incompatible, and she was carrying both of them in her chest like unexploded ordnance as the elevator chimed and the lobby opened around her.

The Vantage Tower lobby was marble and glass and the kind of hush that money bought. Aline crossed it quickly, the folder tucked under her arm, her heels clicking a rhythm that sounded too loud in all that emptiness. She was halfway to the revolving doors when instinct stopped her. She turned.
Up. Forty-seven floors of glass and steel, and at the top, a figure at the penthouse window.
Marcus Webb.

He was standing with his hands clasped behind his back, looking down at her. From this distance, he was a silhouette — dark shape against the morning light — but she could feel the precision of his gaze as clearly as she'd felt his cold handshake. He raised one hand. Not a wave. A signal. Like he was marking her, tagging her coordinates in some system she couldn't see.
Her phone buzzed.
Not Cass's thread. Unknown number. No prefix, no identifier.
She answered.

"Aline." Marcus's voice, warm and close, as though he were standing beside her and not forty-seven floors above. The same practiced warmth, the same furnace-heat hiding something cold underneath. "I know who's producing your music. I've known for weeks."
The marble lobby tilted. She gripped the folder until the edges bit into her palm.
"Tell the Phantom I said hello."
The line went dead.
She stood in the center of the lobby, the phone still pressed to her ear, listening to the hollow silence of a disconnected call. Above her, the silhouette at the penthouse window had not moved.

He knew. He'd known before she walked in — before the coffee, before the folder, before the careful measured words about someone extraordinary who disappeared. The meeting had never been an offer. It had been a demonstration. He'd let her sit in his office and drink his coffee and feel like she was holding her own, and all the while he'd been watching her fumble around a board he'd already set.
The hunter had found the phantom.
And the phantom didn't know it yet.
